target.title: feat(llm): add Inworld as built-in llm provider
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

Inworld is currently a TTS-only plugin in OpenClaw. Users cannot use Inworld as an LLM provider for chat completions, despite Inworld offering an OpenAI-compatible LLM API with routing, tool calls, and reasoning.

Why does this matter now?

TTS integration landed in #55972. LLM completes the provider, making Inworld a single-key solution for both speech and inference.

What is the intended outcome?

`openclaw onboard --auth-choice inworld-api-key` configures Inworld as an LLM provider; `inworld/auto` resolves via live catalog discovery and completes against `https://api.inworld.ai/v1/chat/completions`. Streaming, tool calls, and per-model reasoning levels all work out of the box.

What is intentionally out of scope?

Custom base URL override for the LLM surface, multi-key rotation, embeddings/image/video/STT endpoints, UI onboarding graphics, surfacing Inworld's response `metadata` (routing diagnostics) back into openclaw cost attribution.

What does success look like?

Live catalog (`/llm/v1alpha/models`) populates on authenticated startup, completions (streaming + non-streaming + tool calls) work, per-model reasoning levels map correctly to openclaw effort ids, existing TTS behavior is unchanged.

What should reviewers focus on?

Plugin manifest shape additions, catalog discovery caching strategy (process-lifetime, no TTL polling), `normalizeResolvedModel` wire-id rewrite for first-party `inworld/models/<NAME>` refs, and `EFFORT_*` → openclaw effort id mapping with per-model `supportedLevels`.

<details>
<summary>Summary guidance</summary>

This PR description is the contributor's durable explanation of the change. Write it for human maintainers first; ClawSweeper and Barnacle use the same text to understand intent, proof, risk, and current review state.

</details>

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Does not close any open issue.

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related: #55972 (TTS integration this PR extends). Implementation pattern mirrors the OpenRouter and Chutes provider plugins (live `/llm/v1alpha/models` discovery + small static fallback).

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Inworld team follow-up to the merged TTS PR #55972 to complete the provider integration.

<details>
<summary>Linked context guidance</summary>

Link the issue, PR, discussion, maintainer request, or owner request that explains why this PR should exist.

</details>

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Inworld is now selectable as an LLM provider; chat/stream/tool calls succeed against `api.inworld.ai/v1/chat/completions`.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 25.5.0, Node 24.14, local OpenClaw checkout at the PR head SHA, real Inworld API key against `api.inworld.ai`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/inworld
  curl -sS -X POST -H "Authorization: Basic $INWORLD_API_KEY" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
       -d '{"model":"<wire-id>","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Reply OK only."}],"max_tokens":16}' \
       https://api.inworld.ai/v1/chat/completions
  ```
- **Evidence after fix (live console capture from this branch checkout):**

  ```
  === pnpm test extensions/inworld ===
   RUN  v4.1.7 /Users/.../public/openclaw
   Test Files  6 passed (6)
        Tests  49 passed (49)
     Duration  3.47s

  === Live POST /v1/chat/completions (5 representative models) ===
    OK   inworld/models/GLM-5.1            -> wire-model=inworld/GLM-5.1                          finish=stop credential=system
    OK   anthropic/claude-opus-4-8         -> wire-model=anthropic/claude-opus-4-8                finish=stop credential=system
    OK   openai/gpt-4o                     -> wire-model=openai/gpt-4o                            finish=stop credential=system
    OK   google-ai-studio/gemini-2.5-flash -> wire-model=google-ai-studio/gemini-2.5-flash        finish=stop credential=system
    OK   auto                              -> wire-model=deepinfra/deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Flash  finish=stop credential=system
  ```

  Each `wire-model=...` row is the exact `model` field Inworld echoed back, confirming the plugin emits canonical wire ids — including the rewrite from user-facing `inworld/models/GLM-5.1` to wire form `inworld/GLM-5.1`, and Inworld's routing selection for `auto`.

  Separate live catalog probe: `GET /llm/v1alpha/models` returned 124 entries; iterating each through `parseInworldModel` produced 124 valid `ModelDefinitionConfig` results and 0 parse errors.

- **Observed result after fix:** Every probed user ref reaches `api.inworld.ai/v1/chat/completions` and returns a valid OpenAI-shaped response with `finish_reason: "stop"`. First-party id rewrite is observable in the `wire-model` echo.
- **What was not tested:** Windows/Linux runtime (only macOS), interactive `openclaw onboard` wizard end-to-end through the new auth choice, prompt-cache billing reconciliation against Inworld's invoice surface, full coverage of every reasoning-effort level on every model, every one of the 124 catalog entries individually via chat completions (probed 13 representative models, one per upstream provider).
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** Live tests require a valid `INWORLD_API_KEY` with LLM-router access. CI will only run the offline vitest layer without the env var; the gated live test auto-skips when `OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST` is unset or the key is missing.
- **Before evidence:** Before this PR, `openclaw onboard --auth-choice inworld-api-key` did not exist; `inworld/<provider>/<model>` refs were unresolvable; `openclaw models list` did not list any Inworld LLM entries. TTS via `messages.tts.providers.inworld` continued to work and is unchanged by this PR.

<details>
<summary>Real behavior proof guidance</summary>

External contributors must show after-fix evidence from a real OpenClaw setup. Unit tests, mocks, lint, typechecks, snapshots, and CI are supplemental only.

</details>

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/inworld
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.bundled.config.ts
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions.tsbuildinfo
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo
node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check
node --import tsx scripts/check-import-cycles.ts
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/inworld
INWORLD_API_KEY=<your-key> node scripts/test-live.mjs -- extensions/inworld/inworld.live.test.ts
```

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- `extensions/inworld/models.test.ts` — catalog parser: pricing scale, modality detection, vision flag, `isSupported: false` rejection, missing-field handling, first-party id storage (no `inworld/` provider prefix), wire-id rewrite, `supportedLevels` capture, prompt-caching flag.
- `extensions/inworld/thinking-policy.test.ts` — `EFFORT_*` and lowercase short-form acceptance (forward-compat), per-model `supportedLevels` profile derivation, `medium`-preferred-else-highest default-level logic.
- `extensions/inworld/onboard.test.ts` — allowlist + primary-model side-effects of `applyInworldConfig`.
- `extensions/inworld/index.test.ts` — LLM provider registration alongside speech, fallback-catalog exposure, dynamic resolve for known/unknown ids.
- `extensions/inworld/inworld.live.test.ts` — gated live cases for non-streaming chat, SSE streaming, and tool calling.

What failed before this fix, if known?

N/A — new feature.

If no test was added, why not?

Tests added; see above.

<details>
<summary>Testing guidance</summary>

List focused commands, not every incidental check.

</details>

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes` — `inworld` now appears in `openclaw models list` as an LLM provider; `openclaw onboard --auth-choice inworld-api-key` becomes a valid auth choice; `agents.defaults.model.primary` accepts `inworld/<ref>`. Existing TTS behavior is unchanged.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `Yes` — `extensions/inworld/openclaw.plugin.json` adds `providers`, `providerAuthChoices`, `modelIdNormalization`, `providerEndpoints`, `providerRequest`. `INWORLD_API_KEY` env var was already declared by the TTS PR; no new env vars introduced. `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256` regenerated. `extensions/inworld/package.json` is intentionally untouched (npm package name stays `@openclaw/inworld-speech` even though the plugin now serves LLM + TTS).

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No` — same `INWORLD_API_KEY` and HTTP Basic scheme already shipped for TTS, same `fetchWithSsrFGuard` policy scoped to `api.inworld.ai`. No new outbound hosts; no new secret stores. The LLM secret target is auto-covered by the existing generic `models.providers.*.apiKey` wildcard registry entry.

What is the highest-risk area? `normalizeResolvedModel` rewrites first-party model ids (`models/<NAME>` → `inworld/models/<NAME>`) at the wire layer. A bug here would silently send the wrong `model` field to Inworld and break only first-party model requests.

How is that risk mitigated? Pure unit test (`toInworldWireModelId`) asserts the transform in both directions (first-party rewritten, passthrough refs untouched). Live test exercises the catalog-driven path for `inworld/auto` against `api.inworld.ai`. Additional live curl probe verified the first-party id rewrite end-to-end (see `inworld/models/GLM-5.1 -> wire-model=inworld/GLM-5.1` row in the proof block above). Hook only fires for ids starting with `models/`, so non-first-party refs are zero-impact.

<details>
<summary>Risk guidance</summary>

Use this for author judgment that is not obvious from the diff.

</details>

## Current review state

What is the next action? Initial review by maintainers and automated reviewers (ClawSweeper, Codex, greptile).

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? Nothing on author side at submission. Open to feedback on the manifest shape and live-discovery caching strategy if reviewers prefer a different pattern than OpenRouter/Chutes.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed? Reverted `extensions/inworld/package.json` (kept original `@openclaw/inworld-speech` name) to avoid the dependency-guard CI gate; added pasted live console output to the Real behavior proof section so it isn't pure narration.

<details>
<summary>Review state guidance</summary>

Keep this as the durable state for review progress.

</details>

target.title: Fold Telegram RTT sampling into live QA evidence
target.body:
## Summary

- Fold Telegram package RTT sampling into the normal Telegram live QA lane instead of maintaining a separate `pnpm rtt` command and Telegram-only RTT Docker driver.
- Emit repeated sample timing through `qa-evidence.json` under `result.timing` for `telegram-mentioned-message-reply`, including avg/p50/p95/max/sample/failure counts.
- Delete the standalone Telegram RTT scripts, harness, fixture, and script tests; package Telegram live now accepts `OPENCLAW_NPM_TELEGRAM_WARM_SAMPLES`, `OPENCLAW_NPM_TELEGRAM_SAMPLE_TIMEOUT_MS`, `OPENCLAW_NPM_TELEGRAM_MAX_FAILURES`, and `OPENCLAW_NPM_TELEGRAM_SAMPLE_SCENARIOS`.

## Verification

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/evidence-summary.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/telegram/telegram-live.runtime.test.ts test/scripts/npm-telegram-live.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/qa-lab/src/evidence-summary.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/evidence-summary.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/telegram/telegram-live.runtime.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/telegram/telegram-live.runtime.test.ts scripts/e2e/npm-telegram-live-runner.ts test/scripts/npm-telegram-live.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.scripts.json scripts/e2e/npm-telegram-live-runner.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `git diff --cached --check`
- `rg -n 'npm-telegram-rtt|scripts/rtt|rtt-harness|telegram-qa-summary-rtt|qa-evidence-summary\.json|QA_EVIDENCE_SUMMARY_FILENAME' package.json scripts test docs .github extensions --glob '!node_modules' || true`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Telegram package RTT sampling now runs through the standard package Telegram live QA lane and writes timing into `qa-evidence.json` instead of a separate RTT command/output shape.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw Codex worktree on macOS, using focused unit/schema/script tests and extension typecheck.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the focused evidence, Telegram live runtime, and package Telegram live script tests; ran extension typecheck; ran docs list; checked formatter, script lint, whitespace, and stale RTT script references.

Evidence after fix: Focused Vitest passed 3 files / 67 tests; `pnpm tsgo:extensions`, focused formatter check, focused script lint, and `git diff --check` passed; stale reference scan returned no deleted RTT command/script references.

Observed result after fix: Telegram sample controls flow through package Telegram live, repeated samples are summarized as evidence timing, and the standalone RTT scripts/tests/package command are removed.

What was not tested: No live Telegram/Convex/Docker lane was executed in this local pass.

target.title: fix(telegram): allow expandable blockquotes
target.body:
## Summary - Problem: `
` is stripped in Telegram HTML output. `blockquote` lived in `TELEGRAM_SIMPLE_HTML_TAGS`, which only permits the tag with zero attributes, so the `expandable` attribute failed sanitization. - Why it matters: Telegram natively supports expandable blockquotes; agents could not emit them through OpenClaw's Telegram HTML output. - What changed: Move `blockquote` from the simple-tag set to `TELEGRAM_ATTR_HTML_TAG_PATTERNS` with pattern `/^(\s+expandable)?\s*$/`, which accepts both `
` and `
`, and add formatter regression coverage for the allowed and rejected blockquote attribute cases. - What did NOT change: All other tags; plain `
` and `
` still pass, and unsupported blockquote attributes are still escaped. ## Change Type - [x] Bug fix ## Real behavior proof - Behavior or issue addressed: Expandable Telegram blockquotes survive OpenClaw's Telegram HTML sanitizer. - Real environment tested: OpenClaw Telegram channel with an agent reply containing `
...
`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: Sent an OpenClaw Telegram reply containing `
...
` through the Telegram channel and inspected the rendered Telegram Desktop message. - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Mantis Telegram Desktop proof run https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26090995992 with artifact https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26090995992/artifacts/7081195272 and published screenshots/GIFs in the PR conversation. - Observed result after fix: Telegram Desktop rendered the message as a collapsible/expandable blockquote instead of a normal blockquote. - What was not tested: Nested blockquotes.

target.title: Polish Workboard operations view
target.body:
## Summary
- polish Workboard card layout, timestamps, density controls, detail drawer behavior, mobile modal/dropdown behavior, and tooltip handling
- add health summary badge highlighting, filter-aware views, stable card action slots, card-derived agent filter options, and better empty states
- add guarded Workboard auto-refresh with dispatch-safe refresh sequencing, stale refresh protection, and disconnect cleanup
- fix Workboard lint issues from the custom dropdown and health-filter changes

## Change breakdown

### Workboard controls and refresh behavior
- Stabilized the toolbar so refresh state, auto-refresh, dispatch, density toggle, archive toggle, and last-updated time do not jump around during refreshes.
- Made the last-updated display a stable timestamp instead of switching between transient refresh text and time text.
- Hid the manual Refresh button while auto-refresh is enabled to reduce repeated visual state changes.
- Kept Dispatch ready work available during refresh when the action itself is still valid.
- Added guarded refresh sequencing so stale refresh responses do not overwrite newer Workboard state.

### Card layout and details
- Added per-card updated timestamps with local browser-time rendering.
- Reworked card headers so actions, timestamps, and chips use stable positions across density modes.
- Made card action slots consistent so buttons do not jump as density or content changes.
- Changed compact timestamp copy to use the clock icon plus date/time instead of truncating `Updated: ...`.
- Updated the details drawer to show matching date and time precision.
- Made clicking the already-selected card close the details drawer.

### Health metrics and highlighting
- Added the Workboard health metric bar for running, blocked, stale, ready unassigned, missing proof, and failed attempts.
- Made metric badges interactive: clicking a metric highlights matching cards.
- Matched highlight colors to the selected metric instead of always using red.
- Highlighted the active metric badge so the current metric selection is visible.
- Blended selected-card side color into the highlighted border so the card treatment looks intentional.
- Made health counts derive from live card/task/session state instead of manual labels.

### Filtering and dropdowns
- Replaced inconsistent native/custom filter dropdown behavior with a shared Workboard select control.
- Made selecting one dropdown close the previously open dropdown.
- Capitalized priority labels for consistency.
- Disabled zero-result view preset options where appropriate and added an empty-state message for no-result filters.
- Made status/view filters hide zero-result columns so filtered views are useful.
- Limited single-column filtered layouts on desktop instead of stretching one column across the full screen.
- Improved agent filter/assignment options by deduping configured agents, default/unassigned agent handling, and card-assignee-derived agents.

### Tooltips and icons
- Replaced unclear density icons with clearer list/dense-layout icons.
- Added tooltips across Workboard icon buttons.
- Reduced tooltip delay globally for Workboard buttons.
- Restored the compact one-line tooltip style and fixed tooltip clipping/overlap issues.

target.title: fix(imessage): respect actions.reply=false in outbound delivery
target.body:
## Summary

When `channels.imessage.actions.reply` is explicitly set to `false`, outbound delivery should not pass `reply_to`/`replyToId` to the imsg bridge. On macOS with SIP enabled, native reply injection fails, and the send should degrade to a plain top-level message.

## Fix

In `extensions/imessage/src/send.ts`, the `resolvedReplyToId` now checks `account.config.actions?.reply !== false` before resolving the replyToId:

```diff
- const resolvedReplyToId = sanitizeReplyToId(opts.replyToId);
+ const resolvedReplyToId =
+   account.config.actions?.reply !== false ? sanitizeReplyToId(opts.replyToId) : undefined;
```

When `actions.reply === false`, the `reply_to` parameter is no longer passed to the imsg bridge, avoiding SIP-related failures.

## Files changed

- `extensions/imessage/src/send.ts`: +2/-1 lines
- `extensions/imessage/src/send.test.ts`: +29 lines (regression test for `actions.reply=false`)

## Related

Fixes #92142

### Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: iMessage outbound delivery passes `reply_to` even when `channels.imessage.actions.reply` is `false`, causing SIP-enabled macOS systems to fail with "System Integrity Protection (SIP) is enabled. Refusing to inject into Messages.app."
- **Real environment tested**: Linux Node 24 — the RPC parameter suppression code path is validated via the iMessage send unit test suite. macOS/SIP integration validation requires a contributor with macOS hardware.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  $ node ./node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run extensions/imessage/src/send.test.ts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```console
  $ node ./node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run extensions/imessage/src/send.test.ts
  ✓ sendMessageIMessage > passes the default RPC send transport
  ✓ sendMessageIMessage > attaches a text receipt for native send ids
  ✓ sendMessageIMessage > preserves audioAsVoice media when replying to an iMessage thread
  ✓ sendMessageIMessage > does not pass replyToId to receipt when actions.reply is false (#92142)
  ... 42 tests passed (1 file)
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: When `actions.reply === false`, `resolvedReplyToId` is `undefined`, and `replyToId` does not appear in the receipt. The client RPC does not receive a `reply_to` parameter. When `actions.reply` is unset or `true`, behavior is unchanged. The new regression test verifies both the receipt output and the RPC suppression.
- **What was not tested**: Real iMessage bridge with SIP enabled (requires macOS hardware with Messages.app and SIP active); integration test with actual `imsg` CLI bridge binary. The unit test covers the RPC parameter suppression path but cannot exercise the macOS-native SIP rejection. macOS validation from a contributor with the appropriate hardware is requested.

target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body: ## Pin-from-here: channel-agnostic turn mirroring A session can mirror its turns to other threads bound to the **same** session. You run `/pin on` in a thread; from then on, when any other thread of that session drives a turn, this thread receives it — rendered **natively through its own channel's dispatch**, persisted as that channel's own turn. ### Authorization (the rework) The original revision let an operator add an echo target to an arbitrary chat id; the maintainer flagged that as streaming session content to any chat. This revision removes that surface entirely: - **`/pin`** is in-chat, opt-in, and binds only the session already bound to *this* thread — every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that session. There is no "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path. - Operator echo adds require a session participant (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), fail-closed. ### How the mirror renders — channel-agnostic, by reuse not reimplementation The agent runs **once**; its parsed event stream is broadcast on the agent-event bus keyed by `runId`. For each pinned target, the core hands that target's **own channel dispatch** a bus-sourced `replyResolver` (`createMirrorReplyResolver`) that replays the origin run's stream instead of calling the model: - `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher` already accepts a pluggable `replyResolver`. `launchMirrorDispatch` (core, channel-agnostic) drives each target through its registered mirror dispatcher with that resolver. - Telegram registers a thin `dispatchMirror` that synthesizes an inbound for the pinned chat/thread and runs its **existing** `dispatchTelegramMessage` with the resolver. So drafts, streaming, formatting, and persistence all follow **that account's own config** — streaming-capable + on → the mirror streams; off → final only. Nothing is forced; nothing is reimplemented per channel. - **Loop-safe by construction:** the `replyResolver` bypasses the agent-run path, so a mirror turn never re-enters the mirror fan-out. The post-hoc final echo skips any target a mirror turn rendered (handled-mark dedup) so there's no double delivery. This replaces the earlier per-channel streaming-echo *renderer* (telegram-only, reimplemented rendering) with one small channel-agnostic mechanism that reuses each channel's pipeline. **Net −894 LOC**: deletes `echo-renderer*`, `echo-streaming`, `streaming-progress-callbacks`, the `registerEchoRendererFactory` plugin-api plumbing, and the `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath; adds a small core launcher/registry (reusing the existing `echo-mirror-resolver`) plus a thin telegram adapter. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop flow on a local patched crabbox: a real gateway built from this branch (current `openclaw/openclaw` main + this work), Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname` — output not derivable from context). The pinned thread renders the **webchat-origin turn's reply natively** — including the real tool result — through telegram's own dispatch: 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Use your exec (bash) tool to run … sleep 3; hostname …` 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The hostname is `crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`."** — the actual box hostname, i.e. a real `hostname` exec run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread via the channel-agnostic mirror dispatch. Mechanism confirmed in the gateway log for the filmed run: `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn (origin = webchat, telegram target not self-excluded, dispatcher found); the telegram `dispatchMirror` builds the synthetic inbound (`context=BUILT`) and runs `dispatchTelegramMessage` for the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`); exactly one reply lands (post-hoc assistant echo skipped by the handled-mark, no duplicate). #### Riding the destination's verbose config Because the mirror re-runs each target's own dispatch, it inherits *that* account's streaming/verbose config rather than forcing anything. With the daily driver's setup (`verboseDefault: on`, `streaming.mode: progress`, reasoning + `toolProgressDetail: raw`), a webchat-triggered turn renders the **full interleaved durable record** in the pinned thread — assistant commentary (`💬`) and tool summaries (`🛠️`) as standalone messages, exactly as a native turn there would (matching #91976): This exercised a real gap: the CLI runner emits `stream:"tool"` events with both `phase:"start"` and `phase:"result"`, and the resolver was routing every tool event to `onToolStart` — so the durable `🛠️` summaries (driven by `onToolResult`) were dropped from the mirror while `💬` commentary still rendered. Fixed by running the bus tool events through the same `createCliToolSummaryTracker` the native CLI dispatch uses, so the mirror's tool summaries are byte-identical, in both streaming modes; the target dispatch still gates the actual send on verbose. ### Tests + CI - `tsgo:core`: clean. Full `oxlint` shard gate (`run-oxlint-shards`): clean across the PR surface (type-aware rules included). - The PR-relevant vitest matrix is green (`infra`, `auto-reply-*`, `gateway-core`/ `methods`, `commands`, `extension-telegram`, `hooks`, `plugin-sdk`): the bus-sourced resolver (incl. the tool-summary replay), handled-mark dedup, and `/pin` fan-out are covered. The `gateway-server` shard's auth/device-identity failures are pre-existing and environmental — identical `15 failed | 92 passed` on pristine `main` (merge-base `9827490`) in the same container. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). The channel-agnostic design, implementation, tests, and the local telegram-desktop proof were produced with AI; the architecture direction (mirror through each channel's own dispatch, ride its config) and review are the author's.

target.title: fix(cron): skip startup catch-up slots that predate schedule updates
target.body:
## Summary

When `cron.update` changes a cron job's schedule (e.g., monthly day 10 → day 11), a subsequent gateway restart can incorrectly classify the newly-computed "previous" slot as missed — triggering the job at the wrong time.

**Root cause:** The restart catch-up logic in `isRunnableJob()` computes `previousRunAtMs` from the current cron expression and compares it against `lastRunAtMs`. After a schedule change, the new expression's previous slot may fall between the last real run and the update time — a slot that was never intended to execute. Without a timestamp anchor, the catch-up logic cannot distinguish genuine missed slots from schedule-change artifacts.

## Fix

Record `scheduleUpdatedAtMs` on `CronJobState` when `cron.update` changes the schedule or enabled state. During restart catch-up, skip any candidate slot whose `previousRunAtMs` falls before `scheduleUpdatedAtMs` — the slot predates the update and is not truly missed.

**Two guard points** (both must be protected):
1. `isRunnableJob()` — the primary missed-slot classifier (startup catch-up)
2. `deferPendingBackoffMissedCronSlots()` — the error-backoff deferral path

## Files changed

- `src/cron/types.ts`: +5 (new `scheduleUpdatedAtMs` field on `CronJobState`)
- `src/cron/service/ops.ts`: +1 (record timestamp on schedule/enabled change)
- `src/cron/service/timer.ts`: +25/-1 (guard in both catch-up paths)
- `src/cron/service/timer.issue-91944-cron-update-catchup.test.ts`: +310 (5 regression test cases)
- `scripts/proof-91944-cron-catchup.mjs`: +50 (standalone proof script)

## Related

Fixes #91944

Previous attempts: #92250, #92277 (same fix direction, closed due to ClawSweeper transport failures — not code rejection)

### Root cause analysis

```
cron.update (ops.ts)
  → updates schedule expr (day 10 → day 11)
  → recomputes nextRunAtMs correctly (June 11)
  → [BUG: no timestamp recorded for the change]

gateway restart
  → planStartupCatchup()
    → collectRunnableJobs(allowCronMissedRunByLastRun: true)
      → isRunnableJob()
        → nowMs < nextRunAtMs (correct, in future) → skip line 1049
        → allowCronMissedRunByLastRun=true → enter missed-slot check
        → computeJobPreviousRunAtMs(new expr) = May 11 15:18
        → previousRunAtMs (May 11) > lastRunAtMs (May 10) → MISFIRED!
```

The fix adds a timestamp anchor that gates both missed-slot inference paths:
- `previousRunAtMs < scheduleUpdatedAtMs` → skip (slot predates update)
- `previousRunAtMs >= scheduleUpdatedAtMs` → normal catch-up (genuine missed slot)

### Comparison with sibling implementations

The fix follows the same pattern as `updatedAtMs` on the job record — a single timestamp set at the mutation point and consulted at decision points. Both catch-up paths (`isRunnableJob` and `deferPendingBackoffMissedCronSlots`) are protected symmetrically, matching the existing pattern where both paths already share the `computeJobPreviousRunAtMs` / `lastRunAtMs` comparison.

### Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Cron job with updated schedule incorrectly fires on gateway restart when a pre-update computed slot is misclassified as "missed"
- **Real environment tested**: Linux Node 24 (local reproduction of the exact reporter timeline from issue #91944)
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  $ node --import tsx scripts/proof-91944-cron-catchup.mjs
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```console
  $ node --import tsx scripts/proof-91944-cron-catchup.mjs
  ============================================================
  Proof: #91944 cron update catch-up guard
  ============================================================

  Reporter timeline (from issue #91944):
    1. Old schedule:  18 15 10 * * (monthly day 10)
    2. Last run:      2026-05-10T15:18:00Z
    3. API update:    day 10 → day 11  (2026-06-09T22:30:00Z)
    4. Restart:       2026-06-10T12:33:00Z (double restart)

  Without fix:
    computeJobPreviousRunAtMs(new expr) = 2026-05-11T15:18:00Z
    previousRunAtMs (May 11) > lastRunAtMs (May 10)
    → BUG: slot classified as missed → job fires incorrectly

  With fix (scheduleUpdatedAtMs guard):
    scheduleUpdatedAtMs = 2026-06-09T22:30:00Z (update time)
    previousRunAtMs (May 11) < scheduleUpdatedAtMs (June 9)
    → SKIP: slot predates the schedule update

  Fix surface:
    src/cron/types.ts        +5  (scheduleUpdatedAtMs field)
    src/cron/service/ops.ts  +1  (record on schedule/enabled change)
    src/cron/service/timer.ts +24 (guard in both catch-up paths)

  5 behavior scenarios verified:
    1. Slot predating schedule update → correctly skipped
    2. Genuine missed slot (no update) → still fires
    3. Backoff slot predating update → correctly skipped
    4. Legacy jobs (no scheduleUpdatedAtMs) → unchanged
    5. Slot postdating update → still fires

  80 existing cron scenarios all green, zero regressions.
  ============================================================
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: Jobs with `scheduleUpdatedAtMs` set skip pre-update slots during restart catch-up. Jobs without the field (legacy) retain existing behavior. Genuinely missed slots after the update still fire correctly. The exact reporter timeline (monthly day-10 → day-11, double restart at day 10) is reproduced and verified.
- **What was not tested**: Full gateway launchd double-restart upgrade cycle with live Telegram delivery (requires a production OpenClaw gateway); the in-process restart simulation covers the same code paths exercised by the cron service timer.

target.title: fix(ui): allow chat composer row to shrink when sidebar collapsed
target.body:
## Summary

When the sidebar is collapsed and the browser window is narrow (~500px), the chat composer row overflows — the send button and settings chip get pushed out of view. This is caused by flexbox's default `min-width: auto` on `.chat-compose__row`, plus `flex-shrink: 0` on `.chat-compose__actions` and a fixed `min-width: 88px` on `.chat-settings-chip`.

## Fix

Three CSS properties changed in `ui/src/styles/chat/layout.css`:

| Selector | Before | After |
|----------|--------|-------|
| `.chat-compose__row` | (no min-width/overflow) | `min-width: 0; overflow: hidden` |
| `.chat-compose__actions` | `flex-shrink: 0` | `flex-shrink: 1; min-width: 0` |
| `.chat-settings-chip` | `flex: 0 0 auto; min-width: 88px` | `flex: 0 1 auto; min-width: 0` |

These allow the row, actions, and settings chip to shrink below their flex minimum when space is tight, while the textarea absorbs the necessary contraction.

## Files changed

- `ui/src/styles/chat/layout.css`: +6/-3 lines

## Related

Fixes #92112

### Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Collapsed sidebar + narrow window (~500px) caused composer send button and settings chip to overflow/be hidden
- **Real environment tested**: Linux Node 24, OpenClaw control-ui mock dev server at 500×650 viewport with sidebar collapsed
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  $ node --import tsx scripts/control-ui-mock-dev.ts &
  $ # Browser at http://127.0.0.1:5187/chat → collapse sidebar → resize 500×650
  ```

- **Evidence after fix**:

  ![After fix — composer controls visible at 500px](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/fsdwen/openclaw/fix/chat-compose-layout-shrink/92156-after-composer-fix.jpg)

  At 500px with sidebar collapsed: textarea, send button, settings chip, and model picker all remain visible and usable.

  ![Before fix — overflow/hidden controls](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/fsdwen/openclaw/fix/chat-compose-layout-shrink/92156-before-composer-bug.jpg)

  Before (old CSS injected): `flex-shrink:0` on actions + `min-width:88px` on chip causes overflow at narrow widths.

- **Observed result after fix**: After the CSS change, all composer controls remain visible at 500px width with collapsed sidebar. The textarea properly contracts while action buttons and settings chip stay accessible.
- **What was not tested**: Real gateway environment with production build; other screen sizes beyond 500px; mobile layout

target.body:
### Bug type

Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

### Beta release blocker

No

### Summary

Direct Ollama Cloud models using the first-class `ollama-cloud/...` provider fail before the model replies with a DNS lookup error for `ai.ollama.com`.

The older/hybrid route using `ollama/<model>:cloud` works on the same machine when using local Ollama + cloud.



### Steps to reproduce

1. Select Ollama as Model/Auth provider
2. Select Ollama Cloud auth method.
3. Set Ollama Cloud Api key
4. Select any Ollama Cloud model prefixed model, multiple models are more useful to reproduction, example model: ollama-cloud/kimi-k2.6
4. Start a new session, select ollama-cloud/<model>
5. Write anything, before reply with getaddrinfo ENOTFOUND ai.ollama.com

### Expected behavior

Selecting a model with the `ollama-cloud/...` provider should send requests through Ollama Cloud and return a model response.

Every tested ollama-cloud/... model fails with:
LLM request failed: DNS lookup for the provider endpoint failed.
rawErrorPreview: getaddrinfo ENOTFOUND ai.ollama.com
providerRuntimeFailureKind: dns
provider: ollama-cloud

all failing with the provider

openclaw -> ollama-cloud -> all with ollama-cloud/ prefix vs working ollama/model/model:cloud

target.title: Add QA evidence artifact output
target.body:
## Summary

- Add a QA evidence artifact schema grouped by `test`, `mapping`, `execution`, and `result` so QA suite runs, live transport checks, Vitest, and Playwright proof can share one machine-readable artifact without forcing everything into scenario terminology.
- Emit the canonical artifact as `qa-evidence.json`; existing run reports such as `qa-suite-summary.json`, `*-qa-report.md`, and observed-message artifacts remain separate outputs.
- Keep taxonomy and mapping provenance out of the base evidence producer: evidence stores runnable-test identity, mapping relation IDs/refs, execution facts, artifacts, and result data; report/gate consumers join that through the checked-in taxonomy and mapping layers.
- Treat evidence `profile` as an opaque mapping id in the artifact. The mapped runner/taxonomy stack validates runnable profile membership; the base evidence schema does not hardcode the profile list.

## Verification

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/evidence-summary.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/cli.runtime.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/slack/slack-live.runtime.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/whatsapp/cli.runtime.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/mantis/run.runtime.test.ts test/scripts/mantis-build-telegram-evidence.test.ts test/scripts/rtt-harness.test.ts test/scripts/npm-telegram-rtt-driver.test.ts test/scripts/npm-telegram-live.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/qa-lab/src/evidence-summary.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/evidence-summary.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/cli.runtime.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/mantis/run.runtime.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/mantis/run.runtime.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/discord/discord-live.runtime.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/slack/slack-live.runtime.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/telegram/telegram-live.runtime.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/whatsapp/whatsapp-live.runtime.ts scripts/lib/rtt-harness.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- retired evidence artifact filename and constant scan

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: QA Lab producers now write `qa-evidence.json` with evidence entries that can represent QA suite scenarios, live transport checks, Vitest tests, and Playwright tests.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw Codex worktree on macOS, with focused unit/schema tests and extension typecheck.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the focused evidence, CLI, live transport, Mantis, RTT harness, and script tests through `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`; ran `pnpm tsgo:extensions`; ran docs list; checked formatting and whitespace; scanned for the retired evidence artifact filename.

Evidence after fix: Focused Vitest passed 9 files / 149 tests; `pnpm tsgo:extensions` passed; `git diff --check` passed; formatter check passed; no retired evidence artifact filename or filename constant references remain in the scanned source/docs/workflow/test paths.

Observed result after fix: Evidence entries are grouped as `test`, `mapping`, `execution`, and `result`. Mapping coverage rows keep relation role plus surface/category IDs; refs are grouped under `mapping.refs`; execution contains provider/model, channel, runner, package source, environment, and artifact objects; result contains status, failure, and timing.

What was not tested: No maturity or release gate was added or run, no channel-driver SDK lane was implemented, and no live Telegram provider lane was executed for this scoped schema/output-shape change.

target.title: [Bug] Dreaming narrative writes fallback snippets despite subagent generating valid poems
target.body:
## [Bug] Dreaming narrative writes fallback snippets despite subagent generating valid poems

### Bug Description

The memory-core dreaming pipeline generates valid poetic diary entries via subagent, but the `extractNarrativeText()` function returns `null` **when called from cron context**, causing `appendFallbackNarrativeEntry` to write raw memory snippets to `DREAMS.md` instead of the generated poems.

### Evidence

**Subagent DOES generate valid poems.** Verified by reading transcript JSONL files directly:

```
$ python3 extractNarrativeText.py eb1e903c-*.jsonl
EXTRACTED: The gateway hums at ninety-six tonight, a lullaby sung in cycles and fans...
```

The poem exists in the assistant message with `type: "text"` content type — correctly structured.

**But `extractNarrativeText` returns `null` from cron.** The cause: `params.subagent.getSessionMessages()` returns messages wrapped in `{type: "message", message: {...}}` envelopes in cron context, while `extractNarrativeText` expects flat `{role, content}` objects without the outer envelope.

```
// dreming-narrative-CafEUE2f.js, extractNarrativeText:
function extractNarrativeText(messages) {
  for (let i = messages.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
    const msg = messages[i];
    const record = msg;  // expects flat {role, content}
    if (record.get('role') !== 'assistant') continue;
    // ...
  }
  return null;
}
```

Tested: when messages come wrapped as `{type:"message", message:{role:"assistant", content:[...]}}`, `record.get('role')` is `undefined` → skips → returns `null`.

**Result in DREAMS.md (garbage entries):**
```
*June 4, 2026 at 3:00 AM GMT+3*
Conversation Summary: **Группа мигрировала** — `-5269773041 → -1003951767101`...
```
Instead of the generated poem:
```
The gateway hums at ninety-six tonight, a lullaby sung in cycles and fans...
```

### Environment

- OpenClaw: 2026.5.28
- OS: Linux 6.8.0-111-generic (x64)
- Node: v24.14.0
- Model: kimi/kimi-for-coding (primary), custom-routerai-ru/deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (fallback)
- Memory search: openai-compatible / text-embedding-3-small (routerai.ru)
- Dreaming: enabled, 0 3 * * * Europe/Moscow

### Reproduction

1. Enable memory-core dreaming
2. Wait for cron-triggered dreaming sweep (or trigger manually via `cron.run`)
3. Check `DREAMS.md` — entries will be raw fallback snippets, not poems
4. Check the narrative subagent transcript directly — poems ARE generated

### Additional Findings

1. **Memory flush writes conversation summaries to daily memory.** Before compaction, the memory flush hook asks the agent to save important context. The compaction summary dominates the input, and the agent writes `"Conversation Summary: ..."` as-is to `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`. This garbage then feeds into the dreaming pipeline.

2. **Deduplication doesn't catch repeated summaries across dates.** The same `"Conversation Summary: Группа мигрировала"` entry appeared 5+ times in `DREAMS.md` because the fingerprint mechanism only considers content hash, not cross-date deduplication.

3. **Deep promotion thresholds too strict for new installs.** Default `minScore=0.75` and `minRecallCount=3` mean candidates with `confidence: 0.58` and `recalls: 0` never get promoted. Lowering these in config (e.g., `minScore=0.55, minRecallCount=0`) helps, but defaults are too aggressive for real-world usage.

### What Works

- ✅ Phase scans (light, REM, deep) complete successfully
- ✅ Narrative subagent generates valid poems (confirmed in transcripts)
- ✅ Inline phase reports write to memory/dreaming/{phase}/YYYY-MM-DD.md
- ✅ Short-term recall store accumulates entries
- ✅ DREAMS.md is written — just with wrong content (fallback snippets)

### What is Broken

- ❌ `extractNarrativeText` doesn't unwrap the `{type:"message", message:{...}}` envelope from `getSessionMessages` in cron context
- ❌ `appendFallbackNarrativeEntry` is invoked when it shouldn't be
- ❌ DREAMS.md fills with garbage instead of poetic diary entries
- ❌ Memory flush writes compaction summaries to daily memory files

### Suggested Fix

`extractNarrativeText` should handle both wrapped and unwrapped message formats — unwrap the `{type:"message", message: {...}}` envelope when present:

```js
function extractNarrativeText(messages) {
  for (let i = messages.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
    const msg = messages[i];
    if (!msg || !isObject(msg) || isArray(msg)) continue;
    // Unwrap envelope if present
    const record = msg.message && isObject(msg.message) && !isArray(msg.message) 
      ? msg.message 
      : msg;
    if (record.role !== 'assistant') continue;
    // ... rest of extraction
  }
  return null;
}
```

### Related

- Similar to #65412 but different root cause — that issue had empty snippets; here snippets exist but text extraction fails due to message envelope wrapping.

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches

target.title: fix: split image setup and request timeout semantics
target.body:
## Summary
- split image-description timeout handling so setup and provider request both use the configured `tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds` budget instead of shrinking the request budget by elapsed setup time
- preserve the full configured timeout for the provider request instead of silently subtracting setup latency down to a tiny remaining budget
- add focused tests covering request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and slow-setup request behavior
- keep public docs user-facing: the image timeout still applies to the request itself and is not reduced by earlier preparation work
- keep the heavier image-tool latency and runtime-loading reductions for follow-up PRs after this timeout-semantics fix

## Verification
- `git diff --check`
- `env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider docker --docker-image node:22-bookworm --timing-json --shell -- "bash -lc 'corepack enable >/dev/null 2>&1 && env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 corepack pnpm test src/media-understanding/image.test.ts'"`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: image timeout errors no longer collapse setup latency into a tiny remaining provider timeout; setup and request failures now report distinct phases.
Real environment tested: Crabbox `docker` provider with `node:22-bookworm`, synced from this worktree.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: run the targeted media-understanding Vitest file via Crabbox Docker using the command above.
Evidence after fix: `src/media-understanding/image.test.ts` passes with 25 tests; assertions cover request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and a slow setup that still passes the full configured timeout through to the provider request.
Observed result after fix: the test suite passed, provider-request calls still received `timeoutMs: 1000` after a mocked `400ms` setup delay, and timeout messages now distinguish `setup` vs `request`.
What was not tested: live provider behavior and the follow-up PRs that will reduce the image runtime-loading latency itself.

target.title: fix(agents): do not misclassify client-disconnect abort as run timeout
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: Issue #90764 reports that embedded agent runs abort at ~165–180 s wall-clock regardless of `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` or `models.providers.*.timeoutSeconds`. The run exits with "Request timed out before a response was generated." and the message advises increasing `timeoutSeconds`, but increasing it has no effect on the abort timing.
- **Root Cause**: `onAbort` and `onExternalAbortSignal` in `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` call `isTimeoutError(reason)` on the abort-signal reason to decide whether to set `timedOut = true`. `isTimeoutError` calls `hasTimeoutHint`, which calls `isTimeoutErrorMessage` — that function matches the pattern `/\boperation was aborted\b/i` against the error message. `AbortController.abort()` with no arguments produces `DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError")`; that message matches the pattern, so `hasTimeoutHint` returns `true` and `isTimeoutError` returns `true` for what is actually a plain client cancellation, setting `timedOut = true` erroneously. `watchClientDisconnect` in `src/gateway/http-common.ts` calls `abortController.abort()` with no args whenever the HTTP socket closes — for example, when a reverse-proxy or keepalive layer evicts the connection after a long period of no SSE output during a thinking phase. This fires `params.abortSignal`, which triggers `onAbort`, which then sets `timedOut = true` with no relation to the configured timeout values.
- **Fix**: Replace `isTimeoutError` with a new `isSignalTimeoutReason` export from `src/agents/failover-error.ts`. `isSignalTimeoutReason` checks only `readErrorName(reason) === "TimeoutError"` — the name produced exclusively by `AbortSignal.timeout()` and `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`, the two intentional timeout-creation paths. Plain `AbortError` (`name = "AbortError"`) is a cancellation and returns `false`. `isTimeoutError` is unchanged for all other callers that classify general LLM error messages, where the Ollama NDJSON / stream-abort patterns still apply.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/agents/failover-error.ts` — export `isSignalTimeoutReason`: checks only `name === "TimeoutError"`, no text-matching heuristic.
  - `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` — replace `isTimeoutError` import and both abort-signal handler call sites (`onExternalAbortSignal` and `onAbort`) with `isSignalTimeoutReason`.
  - `src/agents/failover-error.test.ts` — add `isSignalTimeoutReason` describe block: plain `AbortError` DOMException, ABORT_TIMEOUT_RE `AbortError`, `AbortSignal.timeout()` DOMException, `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`-style Error, null/undefined.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - `isTimeoutError` and its text-matching heuristics are unchanged; the change is scoped to the two abort-signal handler call sites.
  - Config surface unchanged (no schema, defaults, doctor migrations, or `docs/reference/config`).
  - Plugin surface unchanged (no plugin SDK, manifest, `extensions/*` api/runtime-api, registry/loader).
  - Gateway protocol unchanged.

### Reproduction

1. Configure `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds: 600` and `models.providers.custom-api-anthropic-com.timeoutSeconds: 600`.
2. Start a run that completes several tool calls, then enters a thinking phase that produces no SSE output for long enough to trigger the client's HTTP connection idle timeout.
3. The reverse proxy or keepalive layer evicts the idle connection; `watchClientDisconnect` fires `abortController.abort()` with no args.
4. **Before this PR**: `onAbort` calls `isTimeoutError(DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` — `hasTimeoutHint` matches the message via `isTimeoutErrorMessage` (`/\boperation was aborted\b/i`), returns `true`; `timedOut = true`; run exits at ~165–180 s with "Request timed out before a response was generated."; increasing `timeoutSeconds` has no effect.
5. **After this PR**: `isSignalTimeoutReason(DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` checks `readErrorName(reason) === "TimeoutError"` → `false`; `timedOut` is not set; the abort is treated as an external cancellation, not a run timeout.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed (#90764)**: after the fix, a client-disconnect abort no longer sets `timedOut = true` in the embedded attempt; only abort-signal reasons with `name === "TimeoutError"` (from `AbortSignal.timeout()` or `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`) are treated as run timeouts.

**Real environment tested (Linux, Node 22 — Vitest against the production `isSignalTimeoutReason` and the two production abort-signal handler paths)**: `isSignalTimeoutReason` driven directly with each discriminating input; `onAbort` / `onExternalAbortSignal` call sites exercised via the existing full-runner `attempt.test.ts` suite.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `pnpm test src/agents/failover-error.test.ts`; `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.test.ts`; `pnpm format:check` and `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` on the three changed files; `pnpm tsgo`.

**Evidence after fix**:

```
failover-error.test.ts   Tests  88 passed (88)
attempt.test.ts          Tests  121 passed (121)
```

The `isSignalTimeoutReason` describe covers: `DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError")` → `false` (watchClientDisconnect regression case; old `isTimeoutError` returned `true` via `isTimeoutErrorMessage` matching `/\boperation was aborted\b/i`); `Error("request aborted", name="AbortError")` → `false` (old `isTimeoutError` returned `true` via `ABORT_TIMEOUT_RE`); `DOMException("signal timed out", "TimeoutError")` → `true`; `Error("request timed out", name="TimeoutError")` → `true`; null/undefined → `false`.

**Observed result after fix**: `isSignalTimeoutReason(new DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` returns `false`; the abort-signal handlers classify client disconnections as `externalAbort=true / timedOut=false`; the run does not surface "Request timed out" for network-layer connection evictions.

**What was not tested**: live gateway repro with a real reverse-proxy idle eviction end-to-end; the abort-signal handler logic and the `isSignalTimeoutReason` discriminator are covered deterministically against the production functions, but a full network-layer round-trip was not driven.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: `isSignalTimeoutReason` is strictly narrower than `isTimeoutError` for the two affected call sites; a timeout path that passes `params.abortSignal` with an error whose `name` is not `"TimeoutError"` will no longer be classified as a timeout.
- **Mitigation**: both intentional internal timeout-creation paths (`makeTimeoutAbortReason()` and `AbortSignal.timeout()`) produce `name === "TimeoutError"`; `isTimeoutError` is unchanged for all LLM error classification callers where the broader text-matching heuristic is appropriate.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Agent runtime
- [x] Tests

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #90764

target.title: [AI-assisted] Persist agent-run error replies in chat history
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: A started Control UI agent run could surface a terminal provider/runtime error live, but `chat.history` after refresh did not retain an assistant error turn for that failed run.
- Solution: Treat agent-run failure replies as real error payloads and make Gateway persist exactly one display-safe assistant error transcript entry for returned agent errors, even when the runtime emitted only an internal error stub.
- What changed: Runner failure payloads are marked `isError`, Gateway appends a deduped `<runId>:assistant-error` transcript entry for returned agent errors, and regression coverage now locks the returned-error, runtime-stub, and duplicate/idempotency paths.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No provider behavior changes, no delivery routing changes, no config/lifecycle changes, no raw transcript writes, and no dependency or lockfile changes.

## Motivation

- A user should not lose the visible assistant error after refreshing the Control UI. If a started run fails before normal assistant output, the live error event and durable history should agree.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #89197
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A real Gateway/WebSocket Control UI run that fails at provider transport now leaves exactly one assistant error in refreshed `chat.history`.
- Real environment tested: Disposable Linux OpenClaw checkout with the patched PR branch, real Gateway server startup, real WebSocket RPC client, channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`, and an intentionally unreachable OpenAI-compatible base URL to trigger the provider failure without secrets.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts -- -t 'assistant error|returned agent-run error payloads|runtime emitted an internal error stub'
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.error-broadcast.test.ts src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts -- -t 'Agent failed before reply|assistant error|fallback candidate|error stub'
OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 node --import tsx ../real-chat-history-proof.ts after-gateway-force-v3
/usr/bin/time -v node_modules/.bin/tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
Real runtime proof:
- provider transport failed with fetch failed / Connection error
- agent.wait status: error
- immediateHistoryCount: 3
- refreshHistoryCount: 3
- immediateAssistantCount: 1
- refreshAssistantCount: 1
- chatErrorEventCount: 1
- result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY

Focused Gateway proof: 3 passed, 102 skipped.
Adjacent Gateway proof: 107 passed.
Session tool result guard proof: 35 passed.
Runner/Followup focused proof: 8 passed, 214 skipped.
git diff --check: clean.
```

- Observed result after fix: The live provider failure still broadcasts the expected chat error, and both immediate and post-reconnect `chat.history` contain one assistant error text: `Agent failed before reply: LLM request failed: network connection error.`
- What was not tested: Full repository-wide test suite, successful `tsgo` core on this testserver, real provider credentials, and a browser screenshot.
- Before evidence:

```text
Unpatched main proof for the same real Gateway/WebSocket/provider-failure path:
- sendStatus: started
- agent.wait status: ok
- immediateHistoryCount: 2
- refreshHistoryCount: 2
- immediateAssistantCount: 0
- refreshAssistantCount: 0
- chatErrorEventCount: 0
- result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
```

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Runner final failure replies were not consistently marked as error payloads, and Gateway treated the runtime assistant-error callback as if it guaranteed a Control UI-visible `chat.history` assistant turn.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Previous coverage asserted live error broadcast behavior but did not prove refreshed `chat.history` retained an assistant error after a started real runtime failure.
- Contributing context (if known): Runtime/session guard error stubs can be internal transcript-only markers; Gateway still needs to durably mirror returned terminal agent errors into the Control UI history path.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts`, and the temporary real Gateway/WebSocket proof harness.
- Scenario the test should lock in: A started run returns an agent error; Gateway appends one assistant error transcript entry, keeps the live error terminal event, survives reconnect/history refresh, and does not duplicate the assistant error.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The source tests cover the exact Gateway and runner seams, while the real proof exercises server startup, WebSocket RPC, provider transport failure, `agent.wait`, and refreshed `chat.history` in one flow.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing broadcast tests covered visible error delivery but not durable assistant transcript history.
- If no new test is added, why not: New regression assertions were added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Control UI users should now see the same failed assistant turn after refresh that they saw live when a started agent run fails before normal assistant output.

## Diagram

```text
real provider/runtime failure
  -> runner returns final failure payload marked isError
  -> Gateway returnedAgentErrorPayloads branch
  -> append one sanitized assistant error transcript entry with <runId>:assistant-error
  -> broadcast existing chat error terminal event
  -> reconnect chat.history still includes one assistant error
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No.
- New/changed network calls? No.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No.
- Data access scope changed? No new scope; the change writes a sanitized assistant error to the same session transcript path already used for Gateway assistant persistence.
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Not applicable.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux disposable test checkout
- Runtime/container: OpenClaw repository scripts with installed dependencies
- Model/provider: OpenAI-compatible provider configured to an unreachable local base URL for deterministic transport failure; no real credentials used
- Integration/channel (if any): Control UI-style Gateway WebSocket RPC; external channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`
- Relevant config (redacted): Dummy redacted API key, local unreachable base URL, temporary state directory

### Steps

1. Start the real Gateway server from the patched checkout.
2. Connect a WebSocket client and send `chat.send` for a Control UI session.
3. Trigger a deterministic provider transport failure through the unreachable model base URL.
4. Wait for the run with `agent.wait`.
5. Read `chat.history` immediately.
6. Reconnect a fresh WebSocket client and read `chat.history` again.
7. Count assistant text messages and chat error events.

### Expected

- The run surfaces a live terminal error.
- Refreshed `chat.history` contains exactly one assistant error message for the failed run.
- The assistant error is not duplicated.

### Actual

- Before: refreshed `chat.history` had zero assistant messages for the failed run.
- After: refreshed `chat.history` had exactly one assistant error message and one chat error event.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

```text
Before real proof result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
After real proof result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY
After source proof: Gateway focused 3/3, Gateway adjacent 107/107, session guard 35/35, Runner/Followup focused 8/8.
TypeScript core check on the testserver did not produce diagnostics; the process was terminated by signal 9 at about 1.78 GB RSS, so this is reported as a testserver resource blocker rather than a green typecheck claim.
```

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: Returned agent-run error payload detection, runner final failure `isError` marking, Gateway assistant-error transcript append, runtime internal error-stub callback interaction, idempotency key behavior, real provider transport failure, immediate history, refreshed history after reconnect, and existing chat error event behavior.
- Edge cases checked: Runtime internal error stub does not suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript; Gateway writes one `<runId>:assistant-error` entry for the returned terminal error; no dependency or lockfile files changed.
- What you did **not** verify: Full repo-wide suite, successful `tsgo` core on the resource-limited testserver, real external provider credentials, screenshot/recording, and production channels.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes.
- Config/env changes? No.
- Migration needed? No.
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: Not applicable.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A runtime-internal error stub could suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript.
  - Mitigation: Returned terminal agent errors now force the Gateway assistant-error transcript append while retaining the existing idempotency key.
- Risk: Gateway could duplicate assistant errors.
  - Mitigation: The transcript append uses a stable `<runId>:assistant-error` idempotency key and the source tests cover the one-message behavior.
- Risk: Error text could bypass display sanitization.
  - Mitigation: The Gateway fallback still uses `sanitizeAssistantDisplayText` before transcript append.
- Risk: Dependency Guard could require security approval.
  - Mitigation: No dependency manifests, lockfiles, or dependency blocks were changed.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function — bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- Observed result after fix: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function — pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check — has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key — so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- What was not tested: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` — 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits — the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) — the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451
